ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for beef teppanyaki; FOODON_03302757 (beef dish) used as the closest broad match. A more specific teppanyaki term does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef meat dishes) is the closest defensible match. No teppanyaki-specific FoodEx2 code exists; broad category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef teppanyaki as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage for specific Asian prepared meat dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beef teppanyaki in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising a portion of sliced beef with bean sprouts and green onion cooked on a hotplate, served as a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Teppanyaki — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Teppanyaki (cr_id F06040009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate beef dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef teppanyaki contains only 0.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g. No direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GI is estimated at approximately 10 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate beef preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (10 × 0.1 × 2) / 100 = 0.02, which is nutritionally negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1034 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid beef teppanyaki as prepared; if consumed, restrict portion to 80–100 g, request low-sodium soy sauce and sauce served on the side, and avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table to keep total sodium intake within daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 0.1 g per 100 g with high protein 12.8 g per 100 g supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef teppanyaki poses minimal glycaemic risk due to negligible carbohydrate content; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total meal carbohydrate from accompanying rice or side dishes, request reduced-sugar marinade to minimise hidden sugars from mirin, and be aware that the very high sodium content may exacerbate diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1034 mg per 100 g and saturated fat from beef contributing to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat beef teppanyaki as an occasional food only; the extreme sodium load (1034 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure and fluid retention risk, while the saturated fat content of beef contributes to LDL elevation — portion control to 80–100 g, low-sodium marinade, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables are strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory red meat (arachidonic acid) combined with sodium 1034 mg per 100 g worsening joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise beef teppanyaki due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from red meat and the extreme sodium content that exacerbates RA-related tissue oedema; substituting with salmon or tofu teppanyaki and requesting dramatically reduced soy sauce is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef teppanyaki is a Japanese-origin dish widely served in Singapore, consisting of boneless beef slices cooked on a high-heat iron hotplate (teppan) together with bean sprouts and green onion, finished with a dark soy-based teppanyaki marinade. The cooking method produces a characteristic seared exterior with a tender, juicy interior, and the marinade imparts a savoury-sweet umami flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 129 kcal, 12.8 g protein, 8.6 g fat, and only 0.1 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate option. Dietary fibre is 1.9 g from the bean sprouts and green onion. The dominant clinical concern is the very high sodium content of 1034 mg per 100 g, driven by soy sauce and teppanyaki marinade, which makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the negligible carbohydrate load poses minimal glycaemic risk, though hidden sugars in the marinade (mirin) and accompanying rice portions should be monitored. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. It is commonly served as a main course at Japanese restaurants, food courts, and hawker stalls across Singapore, often accompanied by steamed white rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef teppanyaki?
Is beef teppanyaki suitable for diabetics?
Is beef teppanyaki high in sodium?
Can I eat beef teppanyaki if I have high blood pressure?
What is teppanyaki beef made of?
